blocks.statement: I can compute W = F·d·cos θ for an angled force, and I can assign the correct SIGN of work for forces that aid or oppose motion.

blocks.whatWeKnow: If 2026-XJ enters our atmosphere, air drag does NEGATIVE work on it — taking energy AWAY.
blocks.connection: That's why meteors GLOW. Their KE is converting to heat and light because atmospheric friction did massive negative work.

blocks.markdown:
## Work at an angle — and work with a sign

Yesterday's W = F·d assumed the force points exactly along the motion. Usually it doesn't. The fix:

**W = F · d · cos θ** — where θ is the angle between the force and the motion. Only the **component of F along the motion** does work.

The cos θ does three jobs at once:

| θ | cos θ | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0° | 1 | force fully helps → full positive work |
| 90° | 0 | force ⊥ motion → ZERO work |
| 180° | −1 | force opposes motion → NEGATIVE work |

Negative work is real work: it **removes** energy from the object. Friction always does negative work on a sliding object — that's where the object's energy of motion goes.

terms.definition: When the force F makes an angle θ with the direction of motion d, only the COMPONENT of F along d does work: W = F · d · cos θ. Pulling a suitcase with a 100-N handle force at 30° above horizontal over 10 m: W = 100·10·cos30° ≈ 866 J. At θ = 90°, cos θ = 0 and W = 0 — even though there's a big force.
terms.cognate: Sp. trabajo con ángulo · Pt. trabalho com ângulo · HC travay ak ang

terms.definition: Work done by a force that opposes the motion (θ between 90° and 180°). cos θ is negative, so W is negative — energy is taken AWAY from the object. Friction always does negative work on a sliding object: cos180° = −1.
terms.cognate: Sp. trabajo negativo · Pt. trabalho negativo · HC travay negatif

blocks.title: "Negative work" doesn't mean "no work"
blocks.markdown: It means energy is being REMOVED from the object, not added. Zero work and negative work are different things.

blocks.prompt: You pull a 100-N handle force on a suitcase 10 m at 30° above the horizontal. Friction opposes the motion with 20 N. Find: (a) work done by you, (b) work done by friction, (c) NET work.
blocks.given: F_you = 100 N, θ_you = 30° · F_fric = 20 N, θ_fric = 180° · d = 10 m
blocks.equation: W = F · d · cos θ (for each force)
blocks.answer: You did 866 J of POSITIVE work. Friction did 200 J of NEGATIVE work. Net: 666 J.

blocks.title: Write the angle FIRST
blocks.markdown: ALWAYS write the angle BEFORE you compute. θ is between F and d, NOT between F and the floor. cos180° = −1, cos90° = 0, cos0° = 1.

blocks.prompt: You push a 30-N box 4 m across the floor at constant speed against 12 N of friction. (a) What work did YOU do? (b) What work did friction do? (c) What's the NET work?
blocks.givenHint: F_you = 30 N at θ = 0° · F_fric = 12 N at θ = 180° · d = 4 m
blocks.equationHint: Compute each force's work with its own angle, then add them (signs and all).

blocks.prompt: You push a 30-N box 4 m at constant speed against 12 N of friction. What's the NET work? Then complete the frame about friction's sign.
blocks.frame: Friction does NEGATIVE work because the friction force points ___ to the motion, so cos θ = ___, so W comes out ___.
